About

I take medical devices from concept to commercialization — building the quality systems that ensure products reach the end user safe, effective, and compliant.

With almost eight years across R&D and Quality, and dual master's degrees in Industrial and Mechanical Engineering, I work at the intersection of engineering innovation and regulatory rigor. My practice spans early-stage design inputs and feasibility through DFMEA, risk management, design verification and validation, process and test method validation, supplier quality, and post-market surveillance.

The work I value most is making complex quality systems better — through rigorous standards application, data-driven decision-making, mentorship, and the kind of cross-functional problem-solving that bridges R&D, regulatory, and manufacturing.

Experience Almost 8 years across R&D and Quality

Practice

From concept to commercialization, with discipline.

My practice covers the full medical device lifecycle — from translating user needs into design inputs through post-market surveillance. Every engagement is grounded in a risk-based approach per ISO 14971, traceability per 21 CFR 820.30, and a clear sense of what good looks like at each phase.

01 — Design Controls

DHF Build, Remediation & D&D Planning

End-to-end design history file authoring under 21 CFR 820.30 and ISO 13485:2016. Design and development plans, user needs capture, translation into design inputs and design outputs, with traceability sustained through verification and validation. Risk-based design controls from concept through commercialization.

02 — Risk Management

ISO 14971 Risk File Architecture

Risk management plans, hazard analyses, FMEA, residual risk evaluation, and benefit-risk reasoning. Coverage extends to biologic-derived materials under BS EN ISO 22442-1 where applicable. Risk files designed to withstand reviewer challenge on scope, traceability, and clinical justification.

03 — FMEA Leadership

UFMEA, DFMEA & PFMEA

Cross-functional facilitation of use-FMEA, design FMEA, and process FMEA. Severity and probability calibration aligned to ISO 14971 principles, with hazards surfaced early enough to influence design architecture — not document it retroactively. Direct feed into design verification planning.

04 — Verification & Validation

Design Verification & Validation

V&V planning, protocol authoring, and execution oversight. Traceability matrices that connect every requirement to a verification or validation activity. Statistical sampling and acceptance criteria defensible under FDA inspection.

05 — Process Validation

IQ / OQ / PQ Protocols

Installation, operational, and performance qualification for manufacturing equipment, utilities, and software-controlled systems. Protocols built execution-ready against ISO 13485:2016 §7.5.6 and FDA process validation guidance.

06 — Test Method Validation

ATMV, VTMV, MSA & Gage R&R

Analytical and visual test method validation. Measurement systems analysis and Gage R&R studies to qualify inspection and measurement processes. Statistical rigor applied to demonstrate that measurement variation does not mask product variation.

07 — Sterilization & Stability

ISO 11137 & Aging Studies

Radiation sterilization validation per ISO 11137, reprocessing validation, and product stability programs spanning accelerated aging and real-time aging studies. Shelf-life claims supported by data, not assumption.

08 — Post-Market

HHE, Field Issues & CAPA

Health hazard evaluations, field issue investigation, complaint trending, and CAPA leadership. Structured root cause methodology — fishbone, 5-Why, designed analytical studies — paired with defensible regulatory communication.

09 — Supplier Quality

Supplier & Tech Transfer

Supplier qualification, change control, PPAP, APQP, and site-to-site transfer of manufacturing and testing processes. Quality plans that anticipate transfer risk rather than absorb it.

10 — Audit Readiness

Inspection & Audit Preparation

FDA inspection and notified body audit preparation. SOP authoring, mock audits, and quality system gap assessments against ISO 13485, FDA QSR, and EU MDR.

11 — SaMD & Digital Quality

Software Lifecycle & Digital Device Controls

Quality support for software as a medical device and software-enabled systems, including software requirements traceability, risk-control linkage, software V&V planning, SOUP assessment, configuration management, release documentation, cybersecurity considerations, usability inputs, and change-impact review aligned with IEC 62304, ISO 14971, 21 CFR Part 11, and FDA software documentation expectations where applicable.

12 — Sustaining Quality

Process Improvement & Production Monitoring

Sustaining quality support for released products through production monitoring, complaint and nonconformance trending, CAPA effectiveness checks, process capability review, yield and scrap analysis, SPC/control-chart interpretation, line-clearance and DHR review, change-control impact assessment, and continuous improvement initiatives that protect product quality while reducing repeat defects and process variation.

Selected Work

Quality systems across the product lifecycle, not just documentation.

My work centers on building inspection-ready quality deliverables that connect user needs, design inputs, design outputs, risk controls, verification, validation, process controls, and post-market feedback into one traceable system. The emphasis is not only on completing DFMEA or validation forms — it is on making the technical file, DHF, DMR, risk file, and execution records tell one coherent quality story under ISO 13485:2016, ISO 14971:2019, and 21 CFR Part 820.

All engagements are anonymized. Product identifiers, client identities, and proprietary methodologies are abstracted to protect confidentiality. Cases describe the class of problem and approach, not specific projects.

Design Controls DHF
21 CFR 820.30

Design control strategy from user needs through design transfer

Design control work is strongest when every requirement has a clear purpose, owner, evidence path, and downstream manufacturing implication. Experience includes supporting design and development planning, translating user needs into measurable design inputs, reviewing design outputs for adequacy, building requirement traceability matrices, supporting formal design reviews, and aligning verification and validation activities to the approved design input set. The focus is to make the DHF audit-ready while ensuring design transfer into DMR, manufacturing procedures, inspection methods, and acceptance criteria is technically complete.

Risk Management ISO 14971
Hazard Analysis

Risk file architecture with traceable risk controls

Risk management is more than a standalone FMEA. The risk file must connect intended use, reasonably foreseeable misuse, hazards, hazardous situations, harms, risk-control measures, verification of risk-control effectiveness, residual risk evaluation, production and post-production information, and benefit-risk rationale where needed. The approach uses a layered structure: a risk management plan, shared hazard analysis, product-specific risk analyses, severity and probability rationale, risk-control verification links, and clear boundary statements when adjacent standards or special material controls apply.

FMEA Facilitation UFMEA · DFMEA
PFMEA

FMEA leadership across design, use, and process risks

FMEA is most valuable when it changes engineering decisions before failures are locked into the design or process. Engagements include facilitating UFMEA, DFMEA, and PFMEA sessions with R&D, medical safety, manufacturing, quality, regulatory, and supplier stakeholders; calibrating severity and probability using medical-device risk principles; separating failure modes from hazards and harms; and ensuring outputs feed design verification, process validation, inspection planning, labeling controls, and residual risk review.

Verification & Validation DVT · V&V
Traceability

Verification and validation planning with defensible acceptance criteria

Strong V&V packages begin with unambiguous requirements and end with objective evidence. The work includes protocol authoring and review, requirement-to-test traceability, risk-based sample-size rationale, acceptance criteria definition, test method readiness, deviation handling, and report closure. The quality objective is to prevent weak links such as vague acceptance criteria, unsupported sampling, uncontrolled test methods, missing linkage to design inputs, or results that cannot support the intended claim.

Process Validation IQ / OQ / PQ
Manufacturing

Process validation packages built for execution and audit defense

Validation packages often fail because the protocol does not clearly separate installation, operational, and performance challenges. The approach includes equipment and utility boundary definition, critical process parameter identification, worst-case or challenge-condition rationale, calibrated instrument review, alarm and interlock checks, recipe or setpoint verification, sampling plans, acceptance criteria, GDP controls, deviation pathways, and final report conclusions that support routine production release.

Test Method Validation TMV · MSA
Gage R&R

Inspection and analytical method readiness before release decisions

Quality decisions are only as strong as the methods used to make them. Work includes test method validation planning, attribute and variable method qualification, measurement system analysis, Gage R&R, accuracy, precision, repeatability, reproducibility, bias, linearity, range, specificity, and method transfer considerations. The goal is to confirm that the method can reliably distinguish conforming from nonconforming product before it is used for release, validation, investigation, or supplier acceptance.

Supplier Quality Transfer
Change Control

Supplier, site-transfer, and manufacturing change quality support

Supplier and transfer work requires more than moving a drawing or work instruction. It includes supplier qualification, approved supplier list impact, incoming inspection strategy, material and process equivalency review, change-control assessment, risk impact review, PPAP or first article planning where appropriate, process capability review, and transfer readiness evidence. The emphasis is to anticipate risk before launch rather than discover gaps during production or receiving inspection.

Investigations NC · CAPA
Post-Market

NC, complaint, HHE, and CAPA investigations under scrutiny

Effective investigations combine technical depth with disciplined documentation. Work includes problem definition using 4W/1H, containment review, lot impact and patient-risk assessment, fishbone and 5-Why analysis, assignable-cause justification, effectiveness checks, complaint trend linkage, health-hazard evaluation support, and cross-functional CAPA ownership. The closure standard is evidence-based: clear rationale, appropriate objective evidence, documented risk impact, and actions that address the verified cause without creating new quality-system gaps.
